title: Galatians: Freedom by the Spirit
objective: Live out Christian freedom through love, Spirit-led holiness, and restored community.

required_passages.text: For freedom Christ has set us free... Walk by the Spirit, and you will not gratify the desires of the flesh... the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace... Bear one another's burdens.

segments.audio_script: Freedom in Christ is not self-rule but Spirit-enabled love.

Listen to Galatians 4-6. Paul contrasts slavery and sonship, urges believers not to return to law bondage, and commands: 'Walk by the Spirit.' He lists works of the flesh and fruit of the Spirit, calling the church to gentleness, burden-bearing, and sowing to the Spirit.
Galatian churches needed practical guidance to avoid both legalism and license.
Gospel freedom produces transformed character and communal care. The Spirit forms Christlike life that law cannot manufacture.
Key themes include adoption, freedom, Spirit-led sanctification, fruit of the Spirit, mutual restoration, and new creation.
Review question: What is the fruit of the Spirit listed by Paul in Galatians 5? Answer: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control.
segments.correct_answer: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control
acceptable_variants: the ninefold fruit of the spirit from galatians 5:22-23
love joy peace patience kindness goodness faithfulness gentleness self-control
galatians 5 — nine qualities produced by the spirit's work in the believer

segments.audio_script: True freedom bears Spirit fruit and serves others in love. Next, Ephesians unveils God's cosmic plan in Christ.

vocabulary.definition: The nine qualities listed in Galatians 5:22-23 produced by the Holy Spirit in the believer; described as singular fruit — a unified character rather than nine separate achievements

The contrasting list in Galatians 5:19-21 of acts produced by sinful human nature; Paul lists fifteen vices to contrast with the Spirit's fruit, framing the Christian life as a choice between two powers
